steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls after "Kyoto" and before "when brothers André and Édouard Michelin created the guide in 1900, it was little more than a marketing gimmick." The left side is an independent clause ("...its coveted 3-star rating being awarded to top restaurants like Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto"). The right side begins with a dependent clause ("when...in 1900") followed by an independent clause ("it was little more than a marketing gimmick"). The two main ideas contrast: the guide is prestigious today vs. it started as a gimmick. The blank must separate the two independent clauses and signal that contrast.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Kyoto; however,: a semicolon correctly ends the first independent clause, and the conjunctive adverb "however" (followed by a comma) signals the contrast. ✓
  • Choice B — Kyoto,: a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — this creates a comma splice, and it also drops the contrast signal. ✗
  • Choice C — Kyoto, however,: "however" after a comma instead of a semicolon still leaves a comma splice between the two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice D — Kyoto: no punctuation at all creates a run-on sentence and omits the contrast signal. ✗
That matches Choice A — Kyoto; however,.
